title: Discrete hazard reframing with survival-consistent features
summary: Pit-on-next-lap is naturally a discrete-time hazard problem: the event can happen once per stint segment, and hazard should rise as tyre age, degradation, and race context evolve.
rationale: Pit-on-next-lap is naturally a discrete-time hazard problem: the event can happen once per stint segment, and hazard should rise as tyre age, degradation, and race context evolve. Survival literature shows person-period hazard modeling often beats simpler static binary framing, and recent F1 pit-stop work found sequence models strong because they exploit temporal dependence.
implementation_hint: Keep the binary target, but engineer hazard-style features per driver-race-stint: laps since last pit, normalized stint age percentile within race, rolling slope of LapTime_Delta and Cumulative_Degradation over the last 2-5 laps, and a post-pit reset flag. Test complementary-log-log or standard GBM/CatBoost on this enriched table without using future rows.
expected_effect: Should improve recall on late-stint positive rows and reduce noisy early-lap positives; likely incremental CV gain if temporal trend features are the missing signal.
risk: A weaker version of sequential modeling was already suggested historically; gains depend on implementing genuinely causal lagged trends rather than more static ratios.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
